See discussions, stats, and author profiles for this publication at: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/268080431 V-Y rotation advancement ﬂap: A metanalysis and systematic review Article  in  European Journal of Plastic Surgery · December 2014 DOI: 10.1007/s00238-014-1021- x CITATIONS 7 READS 1,882 3 author s, including: Utku Can Dölen Saint Francis Hospital 36 PUBLICATIONS   246 CITATIONS    SEE PROFILE All content following this page was uploaded by Utku Can Dölen on 28 F ebruary 2016. The user has requested enhancement of the downloaded file.

# Page 2^page=2
REVIEW V-Y rotation advancement flapK M metanalysis and systematic review Utku Ran Yölen & öezih Sungur & Uğur yoçer Received: ó July a?]× = Accepted: ]× September a?]× = Published online: ]? October a?]× # Springer.Verlag Berlin Heidelberg a?]× Mbstract Local skin flaps are used to close defects adjacent to the donor site/ They are classified according to their method of movement: flaps that advance from its base to the defect (V. Y- Y.V- single.pedicle- and bipedicle advancement flaps+ and flaps that move on a pivot point (rotation- transposition- and interpolation flaps+/ Despite its frequent use- there is not a unique name for V.Y rotation advancement flap; moreover- there is not a flap class called “rotation advancement” in the textbooks/ A systematic review of the literature was conduct. ed using “PubMed” and “Google Scholar” in December a?]°/ We made different searches using keywords “V.Y.S flap-”“V. Y rotation advancement flap-” and “hatchet flap”/ The search was limited to the studies published in English and French/ In total- °] articles were found/ Five articles presenting musculocutaneous hatchet flap- were excluded from the review/ We included aç articles in which cutaneous and fasciocutaneous flaps were described/ We found ]a case series and five case reports presenting “hatchet flap/” Two case series and two case reports about “V.Y.S flap” were reported/ Four case series and one case report about “V.Y rotation and advancement flap” were written/ V.Y rotation and advancement flap were per. formed under different names on any regions of the body by several authors- and none of the authors re. ported flap loss or any dehiscence that resulted with a second surgery/ With the help of this review- we would like to standardize its design and its dissection/ Level of Evidence: Not ratable/ yeywords Local flap / V.Y.S flap / V.Y rotation advancement flap / Hatchet flap / Flap design / Cutaneous / Fasciacutaneous rntroduction A number of wound closure techniques are available to the plastic surgeon/ With the help of technology- our arsenal is now larger than ever/ From simple dressing to nanotechnolo. gy- we have lots of new tools []–×]/ However- their price and the time needed to see the effects limit their usage/ Microsurgery- which turned the reconstructive ladder up. side down- has a solution for any part of the body [á]/ Even though it requires special microsurgical instru. ments and high experience- it is now generously per. formed in most of the hospitals [ç]/ However- in micro. surgery- it is not always possible to close defects with similar texture and color/ This is the point where the local flaps come to the stage for closure/ Local skin flaps are used to close defects adjacent to the donor site/ They are classified according to their method of movement: flaps that advance from its base to the defect (V.Y- Y.V- single.pedicle- and bipedicle advance. ment flaps+ and flaps that move on a pivot point (rota. tion- transposition- and interpolation flaps+ [ó]/ Despite its frequent use- there is not a flap class called “rotation advancement” in the textbooks/ In this article- we reviewed all the articles about V.Y.S flap- V.Y rotation advancement flap- and hatchet flap and wanted to standardize the design of the flap/ In addition- we would like to present its adaptability in every part of the body/ U/ C/ Dölen (*+ Dr/ Abdurrahman Yurtaslan Ankara Oncology Training and Research Hospital- Plastic- Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery Clinic- Demetevler- Ankara- Turkey e.mail: utkuchan@gmail/com N/ Sungur : U/ Koçer Ankara Training and Research Hospital- Plastic- Reconstructive and Aesthetic Surgery Clinic- Ankara- Turkey Eur J Plast Surg (a?]×+ °ó:ç°á–ç×a DOI ]?/]??ó=s??a°ö.?]×.]?a].x

# Page 3^page=3
çaterial and methods Asystematic reviewof the literature was conducted using “PubMed” and “Google Scholar” in December a?]°/ We made different searches using keywords “V. Y.S flap-”“V.Y rotation advancement flap-” and “hatch. et flap/” The search was limited to the studies published in English and French/ The reference list of each re. trieved article was manually searched to find other potentially relevant articles/ In total- °] articles were found/ Five articles presenting musculocutaneous hatchet flap were excluded from the re. view/ We included aç articles in which cutaneous and fasciocutaneous flaps were described/ We found ]a case series and five case reports presenting hatchet flap/ Two case series and two case reports about V.Y.S flap were reported/ Four case series and one case report about V.Y rotation and ad. vancement flap were written/ There were no prospective co. hort studies among these articles/ All these retrospective arti. cles were analyzed- and a qualitative meta.analysis was given in this article/ esults History of the flap V.Y.S plasty was first described by Argamaso in ] ó° at the Annual Meeting of American Society for Aesthetic Plastic Surgery [ö]/ He used bilateral flaps to close the defect and the resultant scar- which was in “S” shape- gave its name to the flap/ He wrote that that flap “adopts some aspects of the double rotation flaps (or S plasty+ and so of the V.Y advancements for the closure of circular defects/” Four years later- in ] óó- Emmett published his clinic’s]a. year.experience of using adjacent triangular flaps with subcutaneous pedicles to close the defects [ ]/ He men. tioned that leaving a skin bridge in the pedicle produced a better.looking scar- broke the scar line- and also stabilized the subcutaneous pedicle/ He called this uni. lateral flap “single triangular flap with a partially cuta. neous pedicle” or generally known as “hatchet flap” because of its final scar/ In addition- he called the bilateral flap “two triangular flaps with partially cutane. ous pedicles” and referred to Argamaso’sarticle that Argamaso named this flap “VYS flap/” In the literature- some authors followed Argamaso and called it VYS flap and other authors followed Emmett and called it hatchet or double hatchet flap (bilateral+/ In this review- we called this flap unilateral or bilateral V.Y rotation advancement flap (V.Y RAF+ in order to standardize the language of the article/ Design of the flap Argamaso started to design the flap by marking the lesion elliptically with the long axis parallel to the relaxed skin tension lines (RSTLs+/ Then the lesion- which was kept in the center of the ellipse- was excised and the triangular areas at the ends were left intact/ Then- one side of a triangle was incised and a small back cut was made/ The opposite side of the other triangle was incised and further flaps were rotated ×á° and advanced toward each other in order to close the defect/ He mentioned that undermining of the flap was not necessary if there was no difficulty on the mobility of the flap/ Donor area was closed in V.Y fashion/ The final suture line was in “S” shape/ Elliptical excision and direct closure use the elasticity of adjacent tissues- and it is only usable if there is excess tissue/ However- if the defect is on a tight area where there is no spare skin- one can use the ends of the ellipse as local flaps/ This is the idea that Emmett based the technique of triangular flaps with subcutaneous pedicles [ ]/ Contrary to Argamaso’s de. sign- he did not put the defect in the center of the ellipse because he was using unilateral flap/ When necessary- a small triangle from the opposite side was excised in order to fit the scars into the RSTLs and also decrease the circumference of the defect/ If it was needed- another small triangle could have been removed in order to prevent buckling where the pedicle turned/ Unlike Argamaso- he preferred keeping the central subcutaneous tissue of the flap intact and as large as possible without compromising the movement of the flap/ If greater mobility was needed- he did not hesitate to cut the skin of the pedicle/ He used Z plasty to close the donor area of the flap where V.Y fashion closure could leave nondesirable scar or could not be achieved due to skin tightness/ He wrote that in bilateral flaps- each flap had a lateral subcutaneous pedicle rather than a central pedicle (Fig/ ]+/ Elshahat used unilateral V.Y RAF on any part of the body []?]/ He classified the hatchet flap into two types/ Type I hatchet flaps had subcutaneous attachments (fascia or muscle+ below the flap and its movement was dependent on the elas. ticity of the skin/ However- it had dual blood supply- one from the perforators of deeper tissues and the other from the skin bridge of the pedicle/ Type II hatchet flaps were completely free from the underlying tissues/ Despite its higher mobility- the flap received blood supply only from the skin bridge of the pedicle (mainly subdermal plexus- known as random pattern+/ While describing the design of the flap- he did not mention the defect width to flap length ratio/ Instead- he wrote that he did a V.shaped marking; one limb starting from the defect and the other stopped at a length of the defect radius/ He added that the width of the pedicle was dependent on the circulation pattern of the flap and the location of the defect/ He kept the pedicle narrower on type I flaps but larger on type II flaps and on extremities (equal to the defect diameter+/ In our opinion- type ç°ç Eur J Plast Surg (a?]×+ °ó:ç°á–ç×a

# Page 4^page=4
I flaps look like Emmett’s design and type II flaps resemble Argamaso’s design/ Abd El.Rahim and Kamal used unilateral and bilateral to correct acquired alopecia/ They defined the following equa. tion for bilateral V.Y RAF: ××(the alopecia width+,a=the sum of both limbs of the V.Y.S flaps []]]/ Demir et al/ used bilateral V.Y RAF for scalp reconstruc. tions/ They draw triangles with a base equal to the width of the defect and the long axis of the triangles was about two times longer than the base []a]/ Gurunluoglu et al/ reconstructed forehead and infraorbital defects with bilateral V.Y RAF/ In their design- flaps had the same width with the defect and the width to length ratio of the flap was ]:]/á []°- ]×]/ Fernández.Calderón et al/ repaired scalp defects with bilat. eral V.Y RAF/ In their design- the length of the flap was approximately ]/á times longer than the diameter of the defect and the width of the skin pedicle was equivalent to the radius of the defect []á]/ Sheridan and Humzah- marked the lesion elliptically and planned bilateral V.Y RAF as Argamaso described []ç]/ They did not give any equation for the design but they said that the back cut was made °?–á? % of the remaining side/ They recommended not to exceed á? % in order not to compromise flap viability- especially at the tip/ Even though Argamosa did not mention the exact length of the back cut- he emphasized “small” to warn against those devastating complications/ They called that closure “dollar plasty” regarding the final shape of the suture line/ Zhi.guo et al/ used unilateral V.Y RAF to reconstruct cheek defects/ They indicated that the ratio of pedicle width to the length of the flap incision ranged between ]:a and ]:° []ó]/ Borman and Maral closed sacral decubitus ulcers with uni. and bilateral V.Y RAF/ In their design- the length of the flap was equal to the diameter of the defect []ö]/ Sowerby et al/ excised ×ö° cutaneous malignant neo. plasms from all over the body and closed the defects with bilateral V.Y RAF [] ]/ Figure a explains their different design/ In our previous study- we used uni. and bilateral cutaneous V.Y RAF in almost every part of the body/ The following equation for unilateral V.Y RAF was defined: the distance between the center of the defect and the flap corner is two times longer than the diameter of the defect/ For bilateral V.Y RAF this distance is ]/á times longer than the diameter of the defect [a?]/ In order to clarify the flap design- we updated our previous design and enhanced it with figures (Figs/ ° and ×+/ The length of the unilateral V.Y RAF is equal to three times horizontal radius of the defect (l−r=°r+/ The length of the bilateral V.Y RAF is equal to twice kig1 : Drawings of uni. and bilateral V.Y rotation and advancement flaps according to the Argamaso’s and Emmett’s articles/ (l ft+ Argamaso’s bilateral flap design for a forehead defect (l+/ Both Argamaso’s and Emmett’s bilateral flap design for an infraorbital defect (r+/ Emmett’s unilateral flap design for a nasal defect (t+/ Emmett’s unilateral flap design for a buccal defect and closure of the donor area with Z plasty (x+/ Emmett preferred to excise a small triangle on the opposite site of the unilateral flap to obtain a smooth scar line/ (right+ Final scars of the flaps kig1 A Sowerby et al/’s bilateral flap design/ Points l are the intersection between the tirtl (defect+ and the horizontll lin dividing the circle into equal halves/ The length of the flap equals the distance between the flap corner (r+ and the point (l+/ They described that the length of the flap was equal to the diameter of the defect/ The limb of the flap was connected to point (x+- which is the intersection of the thirds and the tirtl / The width of the skin pedicle equaled to the radius of the defect (distance −x=r+ Eur J Plast Surg (a?]×+ °ó:ç°á–ç×a ç°ó

# Page 5^page=5
of radius (l−r=ar+/ These formulas can be changed if the horizontal and vertical diameters are not equal to each other/ If the flap is planned horizontally- horizontal radius is used for the equation as well as vertical radius for the vertically planned flap/ The designs described by different authors are approxi. mately similar to each other and the length of flap equals at least the diameter of the defect in both unilateral and bilateral V.Y flap/ There is no consensus on the length of the back cut- but it is recommended by many authors to keep it long enough to increase the mobility of the flap but short enough not to risk flap viability/ Advancement of the flap equals the length of the skin closed by V.Y fashion/ By making the angle of the flap’s corner more acute- primary closure can be facilitated and dog.ear formation can be prevented/ The rotation movement of the flap is ×á° at maximum because of the tension of the pedicle [ö- ]ç]/ It is always wise to plan the flap slightly concave at the corner in order to facilitate primary closure and plan the rest slightly convex to include all the cutaneous perforating vessels []ö]/ The defect that occurs after the ad. vancement is closed primarily (V.Y fashion+/ By making the angle of the flap’s corner more acute- primary closure can be facilitated and dog.ear formation can be prevented/ Case series with more than five patients were put in a table in chronological order (Table ]+/ Reconstructed body parts and the dissection plan of the flap atllp In scalp reconstruction- flaps were elevated in the subgaleal plane []?- ]a- ]á- a?]/ Abd El.Rahim and Kamal also used the same plane for alopecia reconstruction with uni. or bilateral V. Y RAF []]]/ It is also recommended by authors to do incisions parallel to the direction of hair follicles not to cause scatricial alopecia and if possible- include one of the dominant arteries of scalp to the flap/ Despite their high number of patients with a]a scalp reconstructions- Sowerby et al/ did not describe their surgical plane [] ]/ Slt Argamaso used this flap to cover facial defects and he empha. sized that V.Y RAF cause less displacement of the adjacent tissues and therefore asymmetry of the paired structures (oral commissure- eyelids- etc/+ is less likely to happen [ö]/ Emmett planned the incisions according to the RSTLs and pointed out that leaving a skin bridge in the pedicle resulted with a better. looking scar [ ]/ On the face- flaps are elevated superficial to the superficial muscular aponeurotic system (SMAS+/ Sharma et al/ recon. structed a defect measuring ]a× /á cm on the cheek with multiple V.Y RAF [aö]/ Zhi.guo et al/ operated a patients who had cheek defects []ó]/ Gurunluoglu et al/ elevated bilateral V.Y RAF above the SMAS and the frontal muscle to cover supra.eyebrow defects/ They placed the incisions with respect to the RSTLs and the contour line of the eyebrow/ They did a meticulous dissection to protect the supraorbital and supratrochlear nerves []°]/ Sungur et al/ went deeper and included the frontal muscle into the flap by dissecting above the pericranium to cover a gun. shot defect on the forehead [a?]/ Baron et al/ reported their experience of unilateral V.Y RAF on lateral facial region of five patients/ They placed the pedicle of their flaps on the pre.auricular region and on the anterior part of the parotid region where cutaneous perforators were concentrated [aç]/ Reynaud reconstructed nasal defects of °? patients with unilateral V.Y RAF/ He undermined the flap completely and excised a small triangle adjacent to the defect in order to insert the tip of the flap [aó]/ Larger flaps can be planned by placing the back cut to the glabellar area [a ]/ Flap is elevated super. ficial to the perichondrium and periosteum of the nose/ In the glabellar area- the dissection plan can be either superficial or deep to the procerus muscle [a?- a ]/ The left or right trochlear artery can be kept inside the base of the flap in order to kig1 C Our bilateral flap design/ Longer limb of the flap connects to the point (t+- which is the intersection between the tirtl and the lin dividing the circle into two equal halves/ The distance between the corner of the flap (r+ and the projection of the most distal part of the back cut (x+ onto the horizontll lin equals to the radius (r+ of the circle (defect+/ The length of the flap is equal to twice of radius (distance l−r=ar+ kig1 I Our unilateral flap design/ The length of the flap is equal to three times horizontal radius of the defect (distance l–b=°r+/ Back cut can be lengthened to increase the mobility of the flap ç°ö Eur J Plast Surg (a?]×+ °ó:ç°á–ç×a

# Page 7^page=7
increase the blood supply/ In the remodeling period- this flap pulls the tip of the nose and cause upward tip rotation that must be explained to the patient before the surgery/ Pan et al/ used unilateral V.Y RAF in defects of upper and lower eyelid and nasal ala/ They planned their flaps superficial to the subcutaneous tissue with the subdermal plexus intact [°?]/ By contrast- Gurunluoglu et al/ elevated bilateral V.Y RAF above the orbicularis oculi to cover lower eyelid defects []×]/ Kuwahara et al/ reconstructed the skin and vermillion defects of upper lip with unilateral V.Y RAF on six patients/ They placed the suture line on the nasolabial fold to disguise the scar/ They dissected the flap above the orbicularis oris muscle in the upper lip area and the same thickness as the upper lip flap in the cheek area [aa]/ Makiguchi et al/ reported a case report where they covered an ö? % defect of the lower lip with multiple unilateral V.Y RAF and nasolabial advancement flap/ In their design- the vertical length of the defect was nearly the same as that of the skin flap [°]]/ Yu et al/ presented a case report where they reconstructed a defect with a diameter of ]/á cm on the helical rim of the ear/ They planned bilateral V.Y RAF extending along the whole length of the helix and did the dissection above the perichon. drium [°a]/ Head.neck reconstruction was performed on ç patients by Elshahat and on ]á× patients by Sowerby []?- ] ]/ Boxy Abdominal defects were covered with unilateral V.Y RAF- which was elevated superficial to the fascia of the rectus abdominis muscle [a?]/ Elshahat closed the defects of the chest- axilla- lumbosacral region- buttock- scrotum- perineum- and vulva with unilateral V.Y RAF/ He elevated those flaps on the subfascial plane []?]/ Borman and Maral reconstructed ]á sacral decubitus ulcers with uni. and bilateral V.Y RAF- and they harvested the flap above the gluteus muscle []ö]/ Sungur et al/ used the same fasciocutaneous flap for the same reason [a?] and in addition- for the excisional defects of sacral pilonidal sinus [a×]/ Extr mity Lane et al/ covered the donor site of the radial forearm free flap with unilateral V.Y RAF on ×á patients [a]]/ Schwabegger et al/ published a case report in which they covered a shoulder pressure sore (diameter- á cm+ with uni. lateral V.Y RAF/ They elevated the flap above the deltoid muscle and designed the skin pedicle on the posterior part in order to include neurovascular bundle [°°]/ Sungur et al/ reconstructed eight trochanteric ulcers with unilateral V.Y RAF elevated superficial to the fascia of the muscles/ They reconstructed four knee defects with bilateral V.Y RAF elevated above the patellar tendon/ They coveredTable:(continued+FirstauthorYearCountryNameofflapusedinthearticleFlaptypeLocationCauseofthedefectAnesthesiaNumberofpatientsNumberofflapsMeanageofpatients(years+Sizeofthedefects(cm+Sizeoftheflaps(cm+DrainsFollow.up(months+ComplicationsExcisionofmalignantlesionReynaud[aó]] # Page 8^page=8
two tibial defects with bilateral and three heel defects with unilateral V.Y RAF elevated above the periosteum/ They also covered four dorsal hand defects with unilateral V.Y RAF harvested superficial to the paratenon of extensor tendons [a?]/ Tuncali et al/ published their study about the reconstruction of nine fingertip amputations/ They used unilateral V.Y RAF and included the digital neurovascular bundle to the flap [a°]/ Sungur et al/ elevated bilateral V.Y RAF from the volar side of the finger to reconstruct seven fingertip amputations/ Skin pedicles of the flaps were on the lateral sides of the finger- and the flaps were elevated above the paratenon of the flexor tendon by including the neurovascular bundle to the flap [a°]/ Elshahat used unilateral V.Y RAF to cover two femoral- one knee- one plantar- and one fingertip defects and eight trochanteric pressure sores []?]/ Sowerby et al/ reconstructed a] lower and ]ö upper extremity defects with bilateral V.Y RAF [] ]/ Yiscussion Local flaps have an excellent color match that no other flap can compete with/ Local flap surgery is easier and faster than distant and free flaps/ However- designing a local flap is much harder than the surgery/ While planning the flap- a surgeon must think about the viability of the flap- the donor area of the flap- and the shape of the final scar/ For random flaps- there is a width.to.length ratio ranging from ]:a to ]:× depending on the region of the body [°×]/ Although- with the increasing knowledge of perforator vessels- it is often possible to design a local flap based on a perforator artery in any region of the body/ As plastic surgeons- we all desire to close the donor area of a flap primarily/ This is why flaps are harvested from the regions where there is a skin laxity/ What happens if the defect is on a tight area where there is no known artery? V.Y RAF is the saver/ That flap was performed on all the regions of the body by several authors and none of the authors reported flap loss or any dehiscence or skin slough that resulted with a second surgery []?- ]a–]×- ]ö–a?]/ In order to obtain incon. spicuous scars- suture lines are disguised in the RSTLs or among the connection margins of different structures such as the nose and the cheek or the forehead and the hairline/ Despite the fact that it is not always possible to hide the scar- all the authors were satisfied with the final scar of that flap/ Zhi.guo et al/’s article was the only article that executed a questionnaire about the scar and none of the patients complained about the color match []ó]/ Hypoesthesia is an. other big problem for flaps- but it is proved that V.Y RAF is a sensate flap []°- ]á- a?- a°–aá]/ In addition- it is possible to elevate this flap either cutaneously or fasciocutaneously or musculocutaneously [°á–°ó]/ It means that both a defect of excised nevus and a deep cavity of a decubitus ulcer can be covered by the same flap but in different thickness/ All these properties make this flap very versatile- but there is still no consensus on the dimensions of the flap/ While reproducibility is very important in science- we brought the geometrical formula of this flap to the discussion in order to allow surgeons- mostly the inexperienced ones- to design their flaps without hesitation/ Although medicine is not only a science- surgeons can show their artistic skills by adapting this formula according to the elasticity and redundancy of the patient’s skin and to the shape and place of the defect/ Ronclusion Both senior and junior surgeons can perform this flap on any region of the body with confidence/ Despite its frequent use all over the world- V.Y RAF does not have a special name in the nomenclature yet/ Mcknowledgment We hereby thank Nihal Sulkalar Dölen for helping us with her drawings/ Ronflict of interest None/ athical standards The manuscript does not contain clinical studies or patient data/ eferences ]/ Nasir AR- Brenner SA (a?]a+ Think small: nanotechnology for plastic surgeons/ Ann Plast Surg ç (á+:áö?–áöó a/ Dorafshar AH-
